Q1: I tried to keep a pretty structured routine, honestly. Being 29 and having been through the work world, I know that consistency matters. I'd block out about 20 hours a week, usually in the evenings after I'd handled other responsibilities. I'd listen to lectures and podcasts during my commute too since I learn better by hearing things. It wasn't perfect, but I had a system that mostly worked for me.
Q2: I aimed for regular times, but I'll admit deadlines would sometimes throw me off. I preferred studying Tuesday and Thursday evenings and then a bigger chunk on Sundays. When assignments piled up though, that schedule would shift and I'd cram more hours in wherever I could find them. The routine helped me feel less scared about falling behind, which is something I struggled with.
Q3: Usually it was to check if new lecture recordings were posted or to see discussion board activity. I logged in pretty frequently, about 11 times a week on average. Sometimes I'd log in just to make sure I hadn't missed an announcement because that fear of missing something important was always there. The gamified tasks actually got me logging in more often too because I wanted to see my progress and earn those badges.
Q4: Yeah, after the first few weeks it became automatic. I'd wake up, check my phone, then log into the LMS before even having coffee. The gamified elements really helped make it feel like something I wanted to do rather than had to do. Once I saw my streak building up and my points accumulating, it became almost addictive in a good way. It felt less like a chore and more like checking a game I was playing.
Q5: The video lectures were gold for me since I'm an auditory learner. I watched over 130 minutes of video content because hearing explanations just clicks better for me than reading. The discussion boards were also valuable because hearing different perspectives from peers helped concepts stick. The gamified tasks kept me motivated too, turning what could be boring into something more engaging.
Q6: I'll be honest, I didn't complete as many assignments as I should have, only about 44% of them. Some of the reading-heavy resources I'd skim or skip because reading isn't my strongest learning mode. If something felt like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to the exam, I'd deprioritize it. I know that hurt my overall performance, but with seven courses I had to make choices about where to focus my energy.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could immediately see how it connected to passing the course or understanding key concepts. The gamified tasks were great because they gave instant feedback and made me feel like I was accomplishing something. If I could listen to it rather than read it, even better. Anything that felt disconnected from the main learning goals or took too long without clear payoff, I'd question whether it was worth the effort.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't quite afford with seven courses on my plate. Required ones got priority because they directly impacted my grade. But the gamified optional stuff was different because it didn't feel optional in the same way. It felt like a bonus opportunity rather than extra work. I'd do those when I had energy because they were actually enjoyable, unlike traditional optional readings.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of audio files and listened to them while doing other things, like cooking or exercising. I also used external podcasts and YouTube videos on the same topics when I wanted to hear concepts explained differently. My notes were handwritten because writing while listening helps me process information better. A lot of my actual learning happened with earbuds in, away from my computer entirely.
Q10: There were many times I'd be walking around my apartment, listening to downloaded lectures, pausing to take notes on a notepad. The LMS would show I downloaded something once, but it wouldn't capture the three or four times I replayed sections. Same with study groups over the phone with classmates where we'd discuss concepts verbally. That peer interaction was valuable but completely invisible to the system.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture video or audio file once, and then that file lived on my phone for weeks. I'd revisit it during commutes, while doing chores, whenever I had time. The LMS only saw that single download, but I probably engaged with some of those materials five or six times. It's just more convenient to have things offline when you're always on the move.
Q12: There were definitely moments when fear crept in and made it harder to engage. When I saw my assignment completion was low and worried about failing, I'd sometimes freeze up rather than tackle things. Juggling seven online courses simultaneously was overwhelming at times. When multiple deadlines converged, I'd feel scared about not being able to handle everything and that anxiety would paradoxically make me less productive.
Q13: Fatigue was a real factor, especially being 29 and having more life responsibilities than a typical student. When I was exhausted, I'd still log in but my engagement quality dropped significantly. Competing responsibilities definitely affected my LMS use because sometimes I'd have to choose between the platform and other obligations. The workload across seven courses meant I couldn't give each one the attention it deserved.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd have a video playing but my mind was elsewhere, worried about other assignments or life stuff. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really absorbing what people were saying. Sometimes I'd log in and click around just to feel like I was doing something productive, but I wasn't really there mentally. The scared feeling I often had could make me go through motions without genuine engagement.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments, which is why my completion rate was only around 44%. When something felt overwhelming or I wasn't sure how to approach it, I'd put it off. The fear of doing it wrong sometimes paralyzed me more than motivating me. I'd tell myself I'd get to it after handling something else, and then time would run out. It's something I'm not proud of but it's honest.
Q16: Deadlines were like a fire under me. I'd ramp up my LMS activity significantly when something was due. My login frequency would spike and I'd suddenly be much more focused. Without deadlines, I might let things slide, but knowing a due date was approaching would cut through my procrastination. The gamified tasks helped too because they had their own mini-deadlines that kept me engaged between major assignments.
Q17: Feedback definitely influenced me, though I didn't always get as much as I wanted. When I received positive feedback, it reduced my fear and made me more willing to engage. The gamified system gave constant feedback through points and badges, which kept me coming back. Negative or critical feedback sometimes made me more scared and hesitant to try again, even though I knew that wasn't a productive response.
Q18: The course structure gave me a framework to follow, which I needed. Having clear modules and sequences helped me know what to focus on each week. The gamified elements added motivation that a traditional structure might not have provided. However, when structure was unclear or assignments seemed disconnected from lectures, I'd feel lost and scared about whether I was doing things right.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement much easier because they turned studying into something more interactive and rewarding. The video content was great for my auditory learning style. What made it harder was when there was too much reading-based content or when expectations weren't clearly communicated. Also, having seven courses meant the LMS for this course was competing with six other platforms for my attention.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who logs in frequently, watches videos, participates in discussions, but struggles to actually complete and submit assignments. The engaging part feels manageable, but the producing-work part triggers fear or perfectionism. You can be genuinely interested and active in learning while still struggling with the output side. The LMS activity shows intent and effort, but not necessarily results.
Q21: That makes sense to me because some people are efficient learners who don't need to spend a lot of time on the platform. They might download materials once and study extensively offline. Or they might have strong prior knowledge and just need to review key points. Someone could be very engaged with the course content without that engagement being visible in clicks and logins.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when someone logs in, how long they watch videos, whether they participate in discussions. That's useful data. But they miss the quality of engagement, whether someone is actually absorbing material or just going through motions. They miss offline study, verbal discussions with peers, the emotional state of the learner. They see behavior but not understanding or effort.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material, not just passing. It means thinking about course concepts even when you're not logged in, making connections to other things you know. For me it includes listening actively, participating in discussions meaningfully, and feeling motivated to understand rather than just memorize. It's a mental state more than a set of actions that can be tracked.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I often felt about failing or falling behind. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to downloaded lectures while walking or cooking. It wouldn't show the phone calls with classmates where we worked through difficult concepts together. It also wouldn't show how the gamified elements genuinely improved my mood and motivation, turning fear into something more manageable. The emotional journey is invisible to the data.
